content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660                                322 5901                     322 6441

May 7, 1976


Lawrence H. Bakken
Attorney at Law
765 Lido Drive
Livermore, CA 94550

Dear Mr. Bakken:

     This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation
of Thursday, May 6, 1976. In that conversation, I informed
you that a general law city, like a chartered city, can im-
pose additional requirements other than those contained in
the Political Reform Act so long as the additional require-
ments do not prevent compliance with the Political Reform
Act. See Government Code Section 81013. I also informed
you that the Political Reform Act contains no restrictions
on the amount that can be contributed to a candidate or committee. The Act does contain expenditure limitations, but, in
light of the United States Supreme Court's decision in Buckley
v. Valeo, those limitations are no longer applicable to cam-
paigns involving statewide candidates or statewide ballot
measures. Finally, you asked that I enclose a copy of the
Commission's regulations defining the terms "contribution"
and "expenditures", and I have done so.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kenneth Finney
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

KF:pvp
Enclosures (2)

Lawrence H. Bakken
ATTORNEY AT LAW
765 LIDO DRIVE
LIVERMORE, CALIFORNIA 94550

(415) 447-8721

April 12, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O.Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

RE: Request for Opinion

Gentlemen:

Thank you for sending me Opinion No. 75-125 on the authority
of a chartered city to make a more strict campaign disclosure
ordinance than the Political Reform Act.

Perhaps you already have an opinion, or would prepare one,
on the same issue for a general law city. In particular, may
the City of Livermore adopt an ordinance requiring:

1. More frequent filing periods (than under the Political
   Reform Act).
2. A lower minimum financial disclosure (to report
   every contribution of $1.00 or more).
3. A prohibition on acceptance of contributions after
   a deadline of five days prior to the election date:
   a. Such definition of contribution to include
      anything of value other than money, and to
      apply to legal services given to the candidate
      during this period for the candidate to answer
      a police investigation of violation of local
      election code (i.e. right to an attorney as
      a campaign matter distinct from a service personal
      to the candidate), either before or after the
      election date.
   b. Such prohibition to apply to an individual (i.e.,
      natural person) living, residing, and/or working
      outside of the said city limits, or county, or State.
      I.E., Can a committee register with the FPPC and
      be composed of only people from Oakland and undertake
      newspaper ads, contribute and expend money, etc.
      for a Livermore election without prosecution by
      the County District Attorney acting for Livermore?
      Where does the Livermore jurisdiction stop? Is
      the jurisdiction in personam or in rem?
   c. Such prohibition to include money which is the
      candidate's own money, from his personal estate
      (or that of his spouse, or children, or brother).
4. A prohibition on contributions from business entities,
   e.g., corporation or partnership, or government agency?

- 1 -

FPPC
April 12, 1976                                                    page 2


     5. A requirement for the filing of two copies of each
        campaign disclosure (Form 420 type) if the second
        copy is not sent anywhere.
           a. Enforcement of the actual Form Form 420 of
              the FPPC in two copies, if the second copy
              is not sent to the county clerk or the state
              secretary of state (why two if it just sits
              in city files?)
     6. A limit on aggregate campaign expenses (at, say, 15¢
        per registered voter, for a total of, say, $2550.00).

The matter of re-examining Livermore's election code, Ordinance
No. 875, adopted December 3, 1975, will be on the City Council
agenda on Monday, April 19, 1976. I would appreciate an
answer, or partial answer, by then in order to guide the
review.

                                                  Sincerely,

                                                  [signature]
                                                  Lawrence H. Bakken

Enclosure: Letter, Bakken to Director
           or Finance, dated March 24, 1976

cc: William Parness
    City Manager, City of Livermore
    2250 First Street
    Livermore, Ca. 94550

COPY

FPPC
APR 13 9 11 AM '76

Lawrence H. Bakken
ATTORNEY AT LAW
765 LIDO DRIVE
LIVERMORE, CALIFORNIA 94550

(415) 447-8721
447-1100, ext. 7262

March 24, 1976

Director of Finance
City of Livermore
2250 First Street
Livermore, California 94550

RE: Misdemeanor Violation of Section 2.50.2(d) and 2.52.1 of
    Ordinance No. 875 on Election Campaign Contributions

Subject: Receipt of one million dollars in non-monetary
          contributions (Schedule C) and thirteen cents
          in monetary contributions (Schedule A)

Dear Sir:

Please be advised that I consider Section 2.52.1, absolutely
prohibiting acceptance of any contributions after five days
before the election date, to be unconstitutional and in
violation of the rights of all candidates and their supporters
under the First Amendment of the United States Constitution:

"Congress shall make no law ... abridging the
freedom of speech, ... or the right of the people
peaceably to assemble, and to petition the
Government for a redress of grievances." (Emphasis
supplied).

This includes the political process of elections. In order
to make a test case of accepting contributions of money or
services within five days of election, I present you with
the following facts as contained in my filing of the final
filing of Committee Campaign Statement for Dick Wright,
dated March 24, 1976.

1. Non-monetary Contribution (Schedule C)

Candidate Wright was contacted by the Livermore Police
Department in a City Council directed probe of alleged
election code violations. An appointment for an interview
was made for noon on March 1, 1976, the day before the election.
Wright asked me to accompany him as his counsel, as everyone
has a right to an attorney when under police interrogation.

Page 1

Director of Finance
March 24, 1976                                                Page 2


     When we arrived for the noontime appointment, the
police officer had gone to lunch. Mr. Wright asked me,
"Should we wait for the officer to return?" I said, "It
is not our fault that we have been stood up. I left a
statement at the desk, and if the police want you to come
back, they can always phone again. Let's go to lunch
ourselves!" Wright responded, "That's a million dollar
idea!", and we departed.

     I thus put the value of one million dollars on
this donation of legal services to the candidate, Schedule
C for non-monetary contributions. Now, Section 2.50.2(d)
requires that this contribution be placed in the General
Fund. How do you want to do this?

     Please ask the City Attorney for a legal opinion
as to whether you can accept this individually, whether
you can assign to an agent for acceptance, or whether the
whole city, en masse, gathered at some park at noon can
receive equivalent advice, i.e. "It's noon, let's eat
lunch"! If I am indisposed with laryngitis, will I be
prosecuted for a misdemeanor?

2. Monetary Contribution (Schedule A)

     In order to transmit my belief as to this law's
unconstitutionality for services forced involuntarily
upon the candidate, i.e. having to seek legal representation,
I have contributed the vast sum of 13¢ to buy a first class
postage stamp; affixed it to the envelope surrounding this
letter; and have put it in the blue box on the street
corner that everyone hints is a means of communicating with
you obfuscating, double filing, redundant form enmeshed
violators of our political rights.

     I thus cannot turn the 13¢ over to the General Fund -
it has been spent or tranformed. If you wish to begin
criminal action against me for triple the expenditure
involved ($0.39), I will welcome the tool of the power of
subpena to search out the records of other candidates to see
if there is discriminatory prosecution, to bring to court at
no expense for witness fees the political and law enforcement
leaders to testify as to whether this law is reasonable and
enforceable, and to bring to court faculty members of the
Stanford and California law schools to analyze the constitutional
application.

Director of Finance
March 24, 1976                                                Page 3


     I regret to think that it may cost the city and the
county thousands of dollars in trial and appeal costs
to find that 13¢ postage falls within the right to
petition the government for redress.  But my obdurateness
is a step in the right direction to allow adjudication
on special facts that will void the offending law.

     By publicizing this issue it will remove the constitutionally
'chilling effect of prior restraint' of the prohibition on
giving money as a form of advocacy; it will enlighten future
campaigns on the issue of accepting 'real' money contributions
-- at any time -- whether from the candidate himself or
from his supporters.

     I trust the circumstances here will alert the City Council
on the need to reform this election code and re-write it
within First Amendment rights of free speech and political
advocacy.  Such review should also apply to the horrendous
duplication of State FPPC and City Clerk filing forms with
one standard form and one series of filing deadlines that
will relieve volunteer treasurers such as I from this
"Land of Oz" record keeping of minuscule contributions.

                                            Sincerely,

                                            [signature]
                                            Larry Bakken, Treasurer
                                            Citizens for Dick Wright

CC: Dick Wright, Candidate
    Art Hoppe, Satirist
    Bob Logan, City Attorney

sections.conclusion: In that conversation, I informed you that a general law city, like a chartered city, can impose additional requirements other than those contained in the Political Reform Act so long as the additional requirements do not prevent compliance with the Political Reform Act. See Government Code Section 81013. I also informed you that the Political Reform Act contains no restrictions on the amount that can be contributed to a candidate or committee. The Act does contain expenditure limitations, but, in light of the United States Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo, those limitations are no longer applicable to campaigns involving statewide candidates or statewide ballot measures.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a general law city impose additional campaign finance requirements beyond those in the Political Reform Act, including more frequent filing periods, lower disclosure thresholds, contribution deadlines, contribution source restrictions, and aggregate expenditure limits?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: General law cities may impose additional campaign finance requirements beyond the Political Reform Act provided they do not prevent compliance with state law. However, the Political Reform Act imposes no contribution limits, and its expenditure limitations are no longer applicable to statewide campaigns following Buckley v. Valeo.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains three related items: (1) FPPC response letter dated May 7, 1976 confirming telephone conversation; (2) incoming request from Bakken dated April 12, 1976 asking six specific questions about Livermore ordinance authority; (3) earlier letter from Bakken dated March 24, 1976 challenging local election code constitutionality. Primary focus is the FPPC response letter which is the formal advice letter. Extracted from the response portion as specified.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can a general law city impose additional campaign finance requirements beyond those in the Political Reform Act, including more frequent filing periods, lower disclosure thresholds, contribution deadlines, contribution source restrictions, and aggregate expenditure limits?

CONCLUSION: In that conversation, I informed you that a general law city, like a chartered city, can impose additional requirements other than those contained in the Political Reform Act so long as the additional requirements do not prevent compliance with the Political Reform Act. See Government Code Section 81013. I also informed you that the Political Reform Act contains no restrictions on the amount that can be contributed to a candidate or committee. The Act does contain expenditure limitations, but, in light of the United States Supreme Court's decision in Buckley v. Valeo, those limitations are no longer applicable to campaigns involving statewide candidates or statewide ballot measures.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Kenneth Finney confirms in a May 7, 1976 letter to attorney Lawrence Bakken that general law cities may adopt stricter campaign finance ordinances than state law, clarifies that contribution amounts are unrestricted, and notes that expenditure limitations are unenforceable for statewide campaigns. The letter enclosures include FPPC regulations defining contribution and expenditure terms.
